# Generated by ollama (starcoder2)
# Prompt: Write a Deployment Manager template for a complete GCP application stack (Compute Engine, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Load Balancer). Focus on core infrastructure. Skip Cloud Logging and Monitoring configuration to reduce complexity in our initial MVP deployment.
# Category: cloud_monitoring

*   Deploy to `us-central1`.

*   Use Google Cloud's [Flexible App Engine](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/) and Google [Cloud Storage](https://cloud.google.com/storage/).

    *   Flexible app engine enables dynamic scale out / in of your instances based on usage. Your instance type is customizable at run time, without provisioning new machines or re-configuring your application. It also removes the need to provision servers for testing.
    *   [Cloud Storage](https://cloud.google.com/storage/) lets you store data with high availability and perform file I/O operations directly from Cloud Services. You can access files stored in Google Cloud Platform services using simple URL endpoints, like a publicly available website.

*   Use Google Cloud's [VPC Networking](https://cloud.google.com/vpc). A VPC network contains one or more subnets that are used to segment resources into smaller, manageable sets (for example, to isolate an on-premises data center from your cloud environment)

    *   GCP Network ACL: defines firewall rules for each VPC.
    *   Subnets: provide a mechanism for separating the networking of your virtual network. They allow you to share network resources between one or more VPCs. You can group networks and subnets using a [virtual private cloud (VPC)](https://cloud.google.com/vpc). A VPC can span regions, and contains multiple [subnets](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-vpc#subnet) that contain private ranges of IPv4 addresses. Subnets divide resources like virtual machines into smaller manageable sets. Each subnet has its own IP range, mask (such as 28 or /29), and default gateway.
*   Use Google Cloud's [Cloud SQL](https://cloud.google.com/sql/) to provide MySQL database services on GCP.

    *   **Cloud SQL** is a fully-managed relational database service that makes it easy for developers, DBAs, and DevOps professionals to build & deliver secure apps & databases in the cloud.

*   Use Google's [Google Kubernetes Engine](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/) (GKE) to deploy Docker images of the app to GCP.

    *   Create a cluster based on the GCP [Container Engine](https://cloud.google.com/container-engine/). Container Engine provides a powerful platform for deploying containerized applications and services on Google Cloud Platform.
    *   Use Google's Kubernetes to manage your resources with containers deployed as pods.
        * Pod: collection of one or more Docker containers that share the same pod network namespace (a flat set of IP addresses), and are restarted if they fail.
*   The GKE cluster is installed in a [Network Policy](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/network-policy)
    * Network policies allow you to limit access between containers based on the source IP address of the requests. The rules are evaluated after the application has determined its identity, but before the request is forwarded onto another container in the cluster.
    * Network policy resources in Google Kubernetes Engine must be deployed with a NetworkPolicy resource name that is prefixed with `gcp-policy` to indicate that it is an [Advanced Policy](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/network-policies#advanced_policy) for use with Google's [Advanced VPC Networking](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/) feature.
